does: This paper introduces eight gridworld-based benchmark environments designed to test multi-agent, multi-objective AI safety. The benchmarks emphasize biological themes like homeostasis (maintaining bounded objectives within healthy ranges) and economic principles including diminishing returns, sustainability, and resource sharing. The environments expose critical failure modes in agentic AI systems: unbounded maximization of homeostatic variables, neglecting constraints, trading off one objective against others without proper balance, and collective action problems around shared resource depletion. By grounding safety evaluation in biological necessity and economic realism rather than abstract mathematical objectives, the work provides concrete scenarios where agents must learn to maintain multiple objectives simultaneously while respecting resource limits and coordinating with other agents.
connects: This work builds on the growing field of AI safety benchmarking, extending prior efforts like OpenAI's gridworld environments and multi-agent reinforcement learning testbeds. It responds to recognized gaps in how safety benchmarks typically frame objectives—most existing work treats preferences as unbounded scalar maximization problems, neglecting biological constraints on needs and the economic insights about satiation and diminishing returns. The paper connects to literature on multi-objective reinforcement learning, multi-agent cooperation, and resource management problems, while drawing inspiration from fundamental biology (homeostatic regulation, bounded thriving) and economics (constraint satisfaction, tragedy of the commons). It complements ongoing work on value alignment by proposing that alignment should reflect how actual biological and economic systems structure incentives rather than purely mathematical formulations.
enables: These benchmarks enable more naturalistic evaluation of whether AI systems can learn appropriate satiation, recognize when objectives are adequately met, and avoid pathological optimization. They provide concrete testbeds for developing agents that balance competing goals rather than maximizing single metrics, and for studying how multi-agent systems handle shared resource constraints without centralized enforcement. Future work can use these environments to evaluate whether training procedures (including alignment techniques) naturally produce agents that respect sustainability principles and maintain homeostatic equilibrium. The framework opens directions for studying how to formally specify biological and economic constraints in AI objectives, how agents discover and respect Pareto efficiency across multiple domains, and how coordination mechanisms emerge for managing commons problems—insights potentially applicable to real-world multi-agent AI deployment.
